title: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm
description: A weakening and enlargement of the abdominal aorta that can become life-threatening if it ruptures.
text: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm Vascular Medicine Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 A weakening and enlargement of the abdominal aorta that can become life-threatening if it ruptures. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is An abdominal aortic aneurysm is a permanent enlargement of the aorta in the abdomen caused by weakening of the vessel wall. Risk factors Risk rises with age, smoking history, male sex, family history, high blood pressure and atherosclerotic disease. Symptoms Most are silent. A large or leaking aneurysm may cause persistent abdominal or back pain or a pulsating sensation. Diagnosis and monitoring Ultrasound is commonly used for screening and surveillance. CT imaging helps plan repair. Emergency Sudden severe abdominal or back pain, collapse or shock may indicate rupture and requires emergency care immediately. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. References and further reading CDC and MedlinePlus condition overviews NIDDK endocrine, diabetes and digestive disease resources NHS condition guides Alberta and international emergency-care principles Quick facts Abbreviation: AAA Usually below the kidneys Often symptom-free until large Emergency: In Qatar, call 999 for severe or sudden life-threatening symptoms. title: Alberta Biology 20 and Chemistry 30.
description: MedMinute educational content
text: Academy | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Study › Alberta Biology 20 and Chemistry 30. MedMinute Academy Alberta Biology 20 and Chemistry 30. 11 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 Notes, flashcards, questions and exam preparation. Chemistry 30 has a diploma examination. Biology 20 does not; Biology diploma preparation applies to Biology 30. Biology 20 Unit A Unit A: Energy and Matter Exchange in the Biosphere Study Notes Flashcards Practice Questions Photosynthesis stores light energy in glucose; cellular respiration releases energy from glucose into ATP. Energy enters most ecosystems as sunlight and flows one-way through trophic levels, while matter cycles. Biogeochemical cycles include water, carbon, nitrogen and phosphorus. Gross primary productivity is total photosynthetic production; net primary productivity subtracts plant respiration. Ecological pyramids show energy, biomass or numbers. Energy transfer between trophic levels is inefficient. Click a flashcard to reveal the answer. Biosphere All regions of Earth where life exists. NPP Energy stored by producers after subtracting respiration. Biomagnification Increasing concentration of a persistent substance at higher trophic levels. Carbon sink A reservoir that stores more carbon than it releases. Chemosynthesis Production of organic molecules using chemical rather than light energy. 1. Which process removes carbon dioxide from the atmosphere most directly? Photosynthesis Respiration Combustion Decomposition Show answer Answer: Photosynthesis Photosynthesis converts carbon dioxide into organic molecules. 2. Why is less energy available at each higher trophic level? Energy is lost as heat and through metabolism Atoms are destroyed Consumers stop respiring Producers use no energy Show answer Answer: Energy is lost as heat and through metabolism Organisms use energy for life processes and release much of it as heat. 3. A lake receives excess fertilizer. Which change is most likely first? Rapid algal growth Immediate glacier formation Lower producer biomass No nutrient change Show answer Answer: Rapid algal growth Nutrient enrichment can cause eutrophication and algal blooms. Unit B Unit B: Ecosystems and Population Change Study Notes Flashcards Practice Questions Population size changes through births, deaths, immigration and emigration. Exponential growth occurs under ideal conditions; logistic growth slows near carrying capacity. Density-dependent factors include competition and disease; density-independent factors include floods and fires. Natural selection changes allele frequencies when heritable traits affect reproductive success. Succession describes predictable community change after disturbance. Click a flashcard to reveal the answer. Carrying capacity Largest population an environment can support over time. Biotic potential Maximum reproductive capacity under ideal conditions. Founder effect Reduced genetic variation when a new population begins from few individuals. Primary succession Community development where soil is initially absent. Limiting factor A resource or condition restricting population growth. 1. Which factor is density dependent? Disease transmission Volcanic eruption Drought Hurricane Show answer Answer: Disease transmission Disease usually spreads more easily in dense populations. 2. What is the shape of logistic growth? S-shaped J-shaped forever Perfectly linear Circular Show answer Answer: S-shaped Growth slows as population approaches carrying capacity. 3. After lava creates bare rock, which process begins? Primary succession Secondary succession Artificial selection Biomagnification Show answer Answer: Primary succession Primary succession begins where soil is absent. Unit C Unit C: Photosynthesis and Cellular Respiration Study Notes Flashcards Practice Questions Light-dependent reactions occur in thylakoid membranes and produce ATP, NADPH and oxygen. The Calvin cycle occurs in the stroma and uses ATP and NADPH to build carbohydrates. Glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm; the Krebs cycle and electron transport chain occur in mitochondria. Oxygen is the final electron acceptor in aerobic respiration. Fermentation regenerates NAD+ when oxygen is unavailable but yields little ATP. Click a flashcard to reveal the answer. Thylakoid Membrane structure where light-dependent reactions occur. Stroma Fluid region of chloroplast where the Calvin cycle occurs. Glycolysis Breakdown of glucose into pyruvate in the cytoplasm. ATP synthase Enzyme that uses a proton gradient to make ATP. NADH Electron carrier used in cellular respiration. 1. Where does glycolysis occur? Cytoplasm Mitochondrial matrix Thylakoid lumen Nucleus Show answer Answer: Cytoplasm Glycolysis is a cytoplasmic pathway. 2. What is released when water is split during photosynthesis? Oxygen Nitrogen Methane Ammonia Show answer Answer: Oxygen Photolysis of water releases oxygen. 3. Which stage produces most ATP in aerobic respiration? Electron transport chain Glycolysis Fermentation Carbon fixation Show answer Answer: Electron transport chain Oxidative phosphorylation produces the greatest ATP yield. Unit D Unit D: Human Systems Study Notes Flashcards Practice Questions Digestive organs mechanically and chemically break food into absorbable molecules. Gas exchange depends on concentration gradients, thin membranes and large surface area. The circulatory system transports gases, nutrients, wastes, hormones and heat. Kidneys regulate water, ions, pH and nitrogenous waste through filtration, reabsorption and secretion. Homeostasis uses negative feedback to maintain internal conditions. Click a flashcard to reveal the answer. Alveolus Tiny air sac where gas exchange occurs. Nephron Functional unit of the kidney. Peristalsis Wave-like smooth muscle contractions moving material through the digestive tract. Hemoglobin Protein in red blood cells that binds oxygen. Negative feedback Response that reverses a deviation from a set point. 1. Where is most nutrient absorption completed? Small intestine Stomach Large intestine Esophagus Show answer Answer: Small intestine The small intestine has villi and microvilli for absorption. 2. Which blood vessel carries oxygenated blood from lungs to heart? Pulmonary vein Pulmonary artery Vena cava Aorta to lungs Show answer Answer: Pulmonary vein Pulmonary veins return oxygenated blood to the left atrium. 3. Which nephron process moves useful substances back into blood? Reabsorption Filtration only Excretion Ventilation Show answer Answer: Reabsorption Reabsorption returns water and solutes to the blood. Chemistry 30 Unit A Unit A: Thermochemical Changes Study Notes Flashcards Practice Questions System and surroundings exchange energy as heat and work. Exothermic reactions have negative enthalpy change; endothermic reactions have positive enthalpy change. Calorimetry uses q = mcΔT for heat changes in a substance. Hess's law adds reactions and enthalpy changes because enthalpy is a state function. Standard molar enthalpy is the enthalpy change per mole for a specified process under standard conditions. Click a flashcard to reveal the answer. Enthalpy Heat content of a system at constant pressure. Exothermic Process that transfers heat from system to surroundings. Activation energy Minimum energy needed for an effective collision. Hess's law Overall enthalpy equals the sum of step enthalpies. Calorimeter Device used to measure heat transfer. 1. A reaction warms its surroundings. It is most likely... Exothermic Endothermic At equilibrium only Nonchemical Show answer Answer: Exothermic Heat is released to the surroundings. 2. If 100 g of water increases by 5.0°C, what expression gives q? 100(4.19)(5.0) 100/4.19/5.0 4.19-100 5.0/100 Show answer Answer: 100(4.19)(5.0) Use q = mcΔT. 3. When a chemical equation is reversed, ΔH is... Sign-reversed Unchanged Squared Always zero Show answer Answer: Sign-reversed Reversing the process reverses heat flow. Unit B Unit B: Electrochemical Changes Study Notes Flashcards Practice Questions Oxidation is loss of electrons; reduction is gain of electrons. Oxidation occurs at the anode and reduction at the cathode. Spontaneous galvanic cells convert chemical energy to electrical energy. Electrolytic cells require electrical energy to drive nonspontaneous reactions. Standard cell potential is found from reduction potentials using E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode. Click a flashcard to reveal the answer. Anode Electrode where oxidation occurs. Cathode Electrode where reduction occurs. Salt bridge Completes the circuit and maintains electrical neutrality. Oxidizing agent Species that gains electrons and is reduced. Electrolysis Use of electricity to drive a nonspontaneous reaction. 1. Where does reduction occur? Cathode Anode Salt bridge Wire only Show answer Answer: Cathode Reduction always occurs at the cathode. 2. In a galvanic cell, electrons flow... From anode to cathode From cathode to anode Through the salt bridge only Toward both electrodes Show answer Answer: From anode to cathode Electrons are released by oxidation at the anode. 3. A positive standard cell potential indicates the forward reaction is... Spontaneous under standard conditions Impossible At equilibrium Always slow Show answer Answer: Spontaneous under standard conditions Positive E°cell corresponds to negative ΔG°. Unit C Unit C: Chemical Changes of Organic Compounds Study Notes Flashcards Practice Questions Organic nomenclature identifies the longest carbon chain and functional groups. Important functional groups include alcohols, aldehydes, ketones, carboxylic acids, esters and amines. Addition reactions commonly occur at carbon-carbon multiple bonds. Esterification combines a carboxylic acid and alcohol to form an ester and water. Polymers form from repeating monomers through addition or condensation reactions. Click a flashcard to reveal the answer. Homologous series Family of compounds with the same functional group and general pattern. Isomer Compound with the same molecular formula but different structure. Esterification Reaction of an alcohol and carboxylic acid to form an ester. Monomer Small molecule used to build a polymer. Functional group Reactive atom or group determining characteristic reactions. 1. Which pair can form an ester? Alcohol and carboxylic acid Alkane and alkane Ketone and noble gas Amine and metal only Show answer Answer: Alcohol and carboxylic acid Esterification combines an alcohol and carboxylic acid. 2. Compounds with the same formula but different structures are... Isomers Isotopes Catalysts Electrolytes Show answer Answer: Isomers Structural isomers share a molecular formula. 3. Ethene reacting with bromine is an example of... Addition Combustion only Neutralization Hydrolysis Show answer Answer: Addition Atoms add across the double bond. Unit D Unit D: Chemical Equilibrium Focusing on Acid-Base Systems Study Notes Flashcards Practice Questions Dynamic equilibrium occurs when forward and reverse reaction rates are equal. Le Châtelier's principle predicts how equilibrium shifts after a stress. Equilibrium constants compare product and reactant concentrations at equilibrium. Brønsted-Lowry acids donate protons and bases accept protons. Buffers contain a weak acid/base conjugate pair and resist sudden pH change. Click a flashcard to reveal the answer. Dynamic equilibrium Forward and reverse reactions continue at equal rates. Ka Acid ionization constant. Conjugate base Species remaining after an acid donates H+. Buffer Solution that resists pH change. Equivalence point Point where stoichiometric amounts have reacted in a titration. 1. Adding reactant to an equilibrium generally shifts it... Toward products Toward reactants always Nowhere To zero concentration Show answer Answer: Toward products The system consumes part of the added reactant. 2. A Brønsted-Lowry base is a... Proton acceptor Proton donor Neutron donor Salt bridge Show answer Answer: Proton acceptor Bases accept H+. 3. Which mixture can act as a buffer? Weak acid and its conjugate base Strong acid only Pure water only Strong base only Show answer Answer: Weak acid and its conjugate base A conjugate weak pair can neutralize added acid or base. Exam Preparation Biology 20 Unit & Final Exam Prep Biology 20 is not a provincial diploma-exam course. These are original Alberta-style questions. 1. Why does a food web usually contain fewer organisms at high trophic levels? Show answer Less usable energy is transferred upward. Energy is lost through metabolism and heat. 2. Which structure gives the small intestine a large surface area? Show answer Villi and microvilli They increase absorptive area. 3. What happens to a population above carrying capacity? Show answer Resources become limiting and mortality/emigration may rise. Environmental resistance pushes the population downward. 4. Which molecule is the final electron acceptor in aerobic respiration? Show answer Oxygen Oxygen accepts electrons and combines with hydrogen to form water. 5. What is the primary role of hemoglobin? Show answer Transport oxygen Iron-containing heme groups bind oxygen reversibly. 6. Which process returns nitrogen gas to the atmosphere? Show answer Denitrification Denitrifying bacteria reduce nitrates to nitrogen gas. 7. Where does the Calvin cycle occur? Show answer Chloroplast stroma The light-independent reactions occur in the stroma. 8. What is a key function of the nephron loop? Show answer Create a medullary concentration gradient This allows urine concentration. Chemistry 30 Diploma Prep Original practice based on Alberta outcomes, not secured exam items. 1. A 50.0 g metal sample absorbs 1.20 kJ and warms by 30.0°C. What is its specific heat capacity? Show answer 0.800 J/(g·°C) Use c=q/(mΔT)=1200/(50.0×30.0). 2. Which change increases the equilibrium yield of an exothermic product? Show answer Lowering temperature Removing heat favours the exothermic direction. 3. In a galvanic cell, which species is the strongest oxidizing agent? Show answer The species with the most positive reduction potential A stronger tendency to be reduced means stronger oxidizing ability. 4. What organic product forms from ethanol and ethanoic acid? Show answer Ethyl ethanoate Alcohol + carboxylic acid produces an ester and water. 5. If pH = 3.25, what is [H3O+]? Show answer 5.6 × 10^-4 mol/L [H3O+]=10^-pH. 6. A catalyst is added to an equilibrium system. What changes? Show answer Forward and reverse rates increase equally A catalyst does not change equilibrium position or K. 7. Which electrode is negative in a galvanic cell? Show answer Anode Electrons are produced at the galvanic anode. 8. What is the conjugate acid of NH3? Show answer NH4+ NH3 accepts H+. title: Acne Vulgaris
description: A common inflammatory disorder of hair follicles and oil glands.
text: Acne Vulgaris | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Acne Vulgaris Dermatology Acne Vulgaris 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 A common inflammatory disorder of hair follicles and oil glands. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is Acne involves blocked follicles, excess oil, bacteria and inflammation. Types Blackheads, whiteheads, inflamed papules, pustules, nodules and cysts. Treatment Topical retinoids, benzoyl peroxide, antibiotics, hormonal treatment or isotretinoin may be used depending on severity. Skin care Gentle cleansing and non-comedogenic products are preferable to harsh scrubbing. When to seek help Painful nodules, scarring or significant emotional impact deserve medical care. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. References and further reading CDC and MedlinePlus condition overviews NIDDK endocrine, diabetes and digestive disease resources NHS condition guides Alberta and international emergency-care principles Quick facts Not caused by poor hygiene Can scar Early treatment can reduce impact Emergency: In Qatar, call 999 for severe or sudden life-threatening symptoms. title: Addison's Disease
description: A rare condition in which the adrenal glands do not make enough cortisol and often aldosterone.
text: Addison's Disease | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Addison's Disease Endocrinology Addison's Disease 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 A rare condition in which the adrenal glands do not make enough cortisol and often aldosterone. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is Addison's disease results from damage to the adrenal cortex, commonly autoimmune. Symptoms Fatigue, weight loss, low blood pressure, salt craving, nausea, abdominal discomfort and darker skin pigmentation. Diagnosis Cortisol, ACTH and stimulation testing help confirm adrenal insufficiency. Treatment Patients need lifelong glucocorticoid replacement and often mineralocorticoid replacement, with increased doses during illness as instructed. Emergency Severe vomiting, collapse, confusion or very low blood pressure can indicate adrenal crisis and requires emergency treatment. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. References and further reading CDC and MedlinePlus condition overviews NIDDK endocrine, diabetes and digestive disease resources NHS condition guides Alberta and international emergency-care principles Quick facts Also called primary adrenal insufficiency Requires hormone replacement Adrenal crisis is an emergency Emergency: In Qatar, call 999 for severe or sudden life-threatening symptoms. title: Aortic Dissection
description: A medical emergency in which a tear allows blood to split layers of the aortic wall.
text: Aortic Dissection | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Aortic Dissection Vascular Medicine Aortic Dissection 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 A medical emergency in which a tear allows blood to split layers of the aortic wall. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is An aortic dissection begins with a tear in the inner aortic lining. Blood enters the wall and creates a false channel. Symptoms Classically sudden severe chest or back pain, but symptoms vary and may include fainting, weakness on one side, breathlessness or signs of poor blood flow. Diagnosis CT angiography is commonly used in emergencies. Echocardiography or MRI may be used depending on stability and location. Treatment Dissections involving the ascending aorta usually require emergency surgery. Other dissections may initially be managed with intensive blood-pressure control and close monitoring. Emergency action Call emergency services immediately for sudden severe chest or back pain, collapse or neurological symptoms. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. References and further reading CDC and MedlinePlus condition overviews NIDDK endocrine, diabetes and digestive disease resources NHS condition guides Alberta and international emergency-care principles Quick facts Emergency condition Often presents with sudden severe pain Requires urgent imaging and specialist treatment Emergency: In Qatar, call 999 for severe or sudden life-threatening symptoms. title: Appendicitis
description: Inflammation of the appendix that often requires urgent surgical assessment.
text: Appendicitis | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Appendicitis General Surgery Appendicitis 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 Inflammation of the appendix that often requires urgent surgical assessment. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is Appendicitis occurs when the appendix becomes inflamed, often because its opening is blocked. Symptoms Pain often starts near the navel and moves to the lower right abdomen, with nausea, reduced appetite and fever. Diagnosis History, examination, blood tests and ultrasound or CT may be used. Treatment Antibiotics and appendectomy are standard; selected uncomplicated cases may be managed differently under specialist care. Urgency Untreated appendicitis can perforate and cause serious infection. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. References and further reading CDC and MedlinePlus condition overviews NIDDK endocrine, diabetes and digestive disease resources NHS condition guides Alberta and international emergency-care principles Quick facts Common cause of acute abdominal surgery Pain may move to right lower abdomen Treatment often involves appendectomy Emergency: In Qatar, call 999 for severe or sudden life-threatening symptoms. title: Asthma
description: What happens during an asthma attack and how inflammation and airway tightening affect breathing.
text: Asthma | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Asthma Pulmonology Asthma 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 What happens during an asthma attack and how inflammation and airway tightening affect breathing. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is Asthma causes variable airway inflammation, narrowing and increased mucus. Symptoms Wheeze, cough, chest tightness and shortness of breath, often triggered by infection, exercise, allergens, smoke or irritants. Diagnosis History and breathing tests such as spirometry are important. Peak flow monitoring may show variability. Treatment Reliever and controller inhalers are used according to an individualized plan. Inhaled corticosteroids reduce inflammation. Emergency signs Severe breathlessness, inability to speak normally, blue lips, exhaustion or poor response to rescue medication requires emergency care. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. References and further reading CDC and MedlinePlus condition overviews NIDDK endocrine, diabetes and digestive disease resources NHS condition guides Alberta and international emergency-care principles Quick facts Chronic inflammatory airway disease Symptoms can vary over time Inhaler technique matters Emergency: In Qatar, call 999 for severe or sudden life-threatening symptoms. title: Atopic Dermatitis
description: A chronic inflammatory skin condition commonly known as eczema.

Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is Atopic dermatitis causes dry, inflamed and intensely itchy skin. Triggers Heat, irritants, infection, allergens and stress can worsen symptoms, though triggers vary. Treatment Regular emollients, topical anti-inflammatory treatment and trigger reduction form the foundation. Severe disease may need phototherapy or systemic treatment. Complications Scratching can cause skin infection and sleep disruption. Important note Food restriction should not be started without professional assessment. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. title: Atrial Fibrillation
description: Understanding a common irregular heart rhythm and its relationship to stroke risk.
text: Atrial Fibrillation | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Atrial Fibrillation Cardiology Atrial Fibrillation 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 Understanding a common irregular heart rhythm and its relationship to stroke risk. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is Atrial fibrillation occurs when electrical activity in the upper chambers of the heart becomes disorganized. Symptoms Palpitations, fatigue, dizziness, breathlessness or reduced exercise tolerance. Some people have no symptoms. Diagnosis An ECG confirms the rhythm. Longer monitoring may be needed when episodes come and go. Treatment Management may include slowing the heart rate, restoring rhythm, treating contributing conditions and prescribing anticoagulants for people whose stroke risk is high enough. Why follow-up matters Stroke-prevention decisions are individualized and should never be based on symptoms alone. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. References and further reading CDC and MedlinePlus condition overviews NIDDK endocrine, diabetes and digestive disease resources NHS condition guides Alberta and international emergency-care principles Quick facts Abbreviation: AF or AFib Rhythm: irregular and often fast Can increase stroke risk Emergency: In Qatar, call 999 for severe or sudden life-threatening symptoms. title: Bone Fracture
description: How bones break, heal and are stabilized.
text: Bone Fracture | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Bone Fracture Orthopedics Bone Fracture 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 How bones break, heal and are stabilized. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is A fracture is a partial or complete break in bone caused by trauma, repetitive stress or weakened bone. Symptoms Pain, swelling, bruising, deformity or inability to use the limb. Diagnosis X-rays diagnose most fractures; CT or MRI helps in complex or stress injuries. Treatment Splints, casts, reduction and surgery restore alignment and stability. Emergency An open wound over a fracture, loss of pulse or sensation, severe deformity or possible spine injury requires urgent care. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. References and further reading CDC and MedlinePlus condition overviews NIDDK endocrine, diabetes and digestive disease resources NHS condition guides Alberta and international emergency-care principles Quick facts Healing occurs in stages Treatment depends on alignment and stability Some fractures are emergencies Emergency: In Qatar, call 999 for severe or sudden life-threatening symptoms. title: Brain Aneurysm
description: What a cerebral aneurysm is, why many remain silent and what happens if one ruptures.
text: Brain Aneurysm | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Brain Aneurysm Neurology Brain Aneurysm 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 What a cerebral aneurysm is, why many remain silent and what happens if one ruptures. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is A brain aneurysm is a weakened area of an artery that bulges outward. Symptoms Unruptured aneurysms often have no symptoms, though larger ones may cause headache, eye pain, double vision or nerve problems. Rupture A sudden severe 'thunderclap' headache, neck stiffness, vomiting, collapse or seizure can indicate subarachnoid hemorrhage. Diagnosis CT, CT angiography, MRI or catheter angiography may be used. Treatment Depending on size, location and risk, management may involve monitoring, endovascular treatment or surgical clipping. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. References and further reading CDC and MedlinePlus condition overviews NIDDK endocrine, diabetes and digestive disease resources NHS condition guides Alberta and international emergency-care principles Quick facts Also called: intracranial aneurysm Rupture can cause subarachnoid hemorrhage Often found incidentally Emergency: In Qatar, call 999 for severe or sudden life-threatening symptoms. title: Think through 24 cases.
description: MedMinute educational content
text: Case Challenges | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Study › Think through 24 cases. Medical Case Challenges Think through 24 cases. 5 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 Choose the best answer and reveal the explanation. Case 1 of 24 Score: 0 Endocrinology Case 1 A 15-year-old has excessive thirst, frequent urination, weight loss and fatigue. What is the most likely diagnosis? Type 1 diabetes Asthma Iron deficiency Migraine Check answer The classic osmotic symptoms and weight loss strongly suggest new-onset type 1 diabetes. Urgent glucose and ketone assessment is required. Neurology Case 2 A person suddenly develops facial droop, arm weakness and slurred speech. What is the priority? Wait one hour Call emergency services immediately Offer food Arrange a routine appointment Check answer These are stroke warning signs. Treatment is time-sensitive. Cardiology Case 3 A patient has an irregularly irregular pulse and palpitations. Which rhythm is most likely? Atrial fibrillation Normal sinus rhythm Asystole Complete heart block only Check answer Atrial fibrillation classically produces an irregularly irregular rhythm. Pulmonology Case 4 A teenager has episodic wheeze, cough and chest tightness that worsen with exercise. What test commonly supports diagnosis? Spirometry Bone scan Colonoscopy Skin biopsy Check answer Spirometry with bronchodilator response can demonstrate variable airflow obstruction. Gastroenterology Case 5 Pain begins near the navel and moves to the lower right abdomen with nausea. What is most concerning? Appendicitis Migraine Otitis media Eczema Check answer Migration of pain to the right lower quadrant is a classic appendicitis pattern. Vascular Case 6 An older smoker has a pulsating abdominal mass and sudden severe back pain. What should be suspected? Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm GERD Kidney stone only Anxiety Check answer This combination is an emergency and may represent AAA rupture. Autoimmune Case 7 A young person has joint pain, photosensitive rash, mouth ulcers and protein in urine. Which condition fits best? Systemic lupus erythematosus Osteoarthritis Appendicitis COPD Check answer Multisystem inflammatory findings can suggest lupus and require specialist evaluation. Endocrinology Case 8 TSH is high and free T4 is low. Which pattern does this support? Primary hypothyroidism Primary hyperthyroidism Normal thyroid function Diabetes insipidus Check answer A failing thyroid leads to low T4 and compensatory high TSH. Emergency Case 9 A person with asthma cannot speak full sentences and their reliever inhaler is not helping. What is the best response? Emergency medical care Go to sleep Drink coffee only Wait until tomorrow Check answer Severe breathlessness with poor response to rescue treatment is an emergency. Hematology Case 10 A student has fatigue, pallor, low hemoglobin and low ferritin. What is most likely? Iron-deficiency anemia Hyperthyroidism Stroke Glaucoma Check answer Low ferritin supports depleted iron stores. Neurology Case 11 A patient has the worst sudden headache of their life with neck stiffness. What diagnosis must be excluded urgently? Subarachnoid hemorrhage Tension headache only Eczema UTI Check answer A thunderclap headache can indicate aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage. Nephrology Case 12 A patient has reduced eGFR and albumin in urine for more than three months. What does this suggest? Chronic kidney disease Temporary dehydration only Asthma Acute migraine Check answer Persistent reduced function or kidney damage for at least three months meets CKD criteria. Cardiology Case 13 Chest pressure occurs during exertion and improves with rest. What does this pattern suggest? Stable angina Pneumonia Appendicitis Seizure Check answer Predictable exertional chest pressure relieved by rest can reflect myocardial ischemia. Dermatology Case 14 A teenager has inflammatory papules, pustules and comedones. What is the likely condition? Acne vulgaris Lupus nephritis COPD Epilepsy Check answer Comedones plus inflammatory lesions are typical of acne. Gastroenterology Case 15 A patient has burning behind the breastbone and sour regurgitation after meals. What is most likely? GERD Stroke Fracture Anemia Check answer Heartburn and regurgitation are common GERD symptoms. Mental Health Case 16 Low mood, loss of interest, sleep change and poor concentration have lasted several weeks and impair school. What should happen? Professional mental health assessment Ignore the symptoms Stop all food Self-diagnose online only Check answer Persistent impairment warrants assessment; urgent support is needed if there are safety concerns. Orthopedics Case 17 After an ankle injury, there is deformity, severe pain and inability to bear weight. What is the appropriate next step? Urgent assessment and imaging Continue playing Massage forcefully Wait several weeks Check answer Possible fracture or dislocation requires stabilization and prompt evaluation. Infectious Disease Case 18 Fever, rapid breathing, confusion and low blood pressure occur during an infection. What is the concern? Sepsis Seasonal allergy Acne Migraine aura Check answer Organ dysfunction during infection can indicate sepsis, a medical emergency. Endocrinology Case 19 A person has tremor, heat intolerance, weight loss and palpitations with low TSH and high free T4. What is likely? Hyperthyroidism Hypothyroidism Iron deficiency only CKD Check answer The symptoms and laboratory pattern support excess thyroid hormone. Rheumatology Case 20 Symmetric swollen hand joints with prolonged morning stiffness suggest which disease? Rheumatoid arthritis Osteoarthritis only Appendicitis Asthma Check answer Persistent symmetric small-joint inflammation is characteristic of rheumatoid arthritis. Pulmonology Case 21 Loud snoring, witnessed breathing pauses and daytime sleepiness suggest what? Obstructive sleep apnea Pneumothorax Appendicitis Celiac disease Check answer Repeated airway collapse during sleep causes these symptoms. Gastroenterology Case 22 Upper abdominal pain with black stools is most concerning for what? Bleeding peptic ulcer Migraine Eczema Hypertension only Check answer Melena can indicate upper gastrointestinal bleeding and needs urgent evaluation. Cardiology Case 23 A patient with atrial fibrillation is assessed for anticoagulation mainly to prevent what complication? Stroke Appendicitis Asthma Fracture Check answer Atrial fibrillation can allow clots to form and travel to the brain. Neurology Case 24 Recurrent unprovoked seizures are the defining feature of what condition? Epilepsy GERD Hypertension Osteoarthritis Check answer Epilepsy is a lasting predisposition to recurrent unprovoked seizures. Previous Next Simplified educational cases only. title: Celiac Disease
description: An immune reaction to gluten that damages the small intestine.
text: Celiac Disease | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Celiac Disease Autoimmune & Gastroenterology Celiac Disease 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 An immune reaction to gluten that damages the small intestine. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is Celiac disease is an immune-mediated condition triggered by gluten in genetically susceptible people. Symptoms Diarrhea, abdominal discomfort, bloating, iron-deficiency anemia, fatigue, weight problems or poor growth. Diagnosis Blood antibody testing is performed while the person is still eating gluten. Small-bowel biopsy may confirm the diagnosis. Treatment Strict lifelong avoidance of gluten allows the intestine to heal. Important note Starting a gluten-free diet before testing can make diagnosis harder. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. References and further reading CDC and MedlinePlus condition overviews NIDDK endocrine, diabetes and digestive disease resources NHS condition guides Alberta and international emergency-care principles Quick facts Trigger: gluten Can affect digestion, blood and bones Requires a lifelong gluten-free diet after diagnosis Emergency: In Qatar, call 999 for severe or sudden life-threatening symptoms. title: Chronic Kidney Disease
description: Progressive loss of kidney function and why early disease is often silent.
text: Chronic Kidney Disease | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Chronic Kidney Disease Nephrology Chronic Kidney Disease 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 Progressive loss of kidney function and why early disease is often silent. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is CKD means kidney damage or reduced function persists for at least three months. Symptoms Early disease often causes none. Advanced disease may cause swelling, fatigue, itching, nausea or breathlessness. Diagnosis Blood creatinine is used to estimate filtration; urine albumin detects kidney damage. Treatment Blood-pressure and diabetes control, kidney-protective medicines, avoiding harmful exposures and addressing complications slow progression. Advanced care Severe kidney failure may require dialysis or transplantation. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. References and further reading CDC and MedlinePlus condition overviews NIDDK endocrine, diabetes and digestive disease resources NHS condition guides Alberta and international emergency-care principles Quick facts Abbreviation: CKD Common causes: diabetes and hypertension Detected with eGFR and urine albumin Emergency: In Qatar, call 999 for severe or sudden life-threatening symptoms. title: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease
description: A group of chronic lung conditions that limit airflow, most often linked to smoking or harmful exposure.
text: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease Pulmonology Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 A group of chronic lung conditions that limit airflow, most often linked to smoking or harmful exposure. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is COPD causes persistent airflow limitation from airway inflammation and destruction of lung tissue. Symptoms Progressive breathlessness, chronic cough, sputum and repeated chest infections. Diagnosis Post-bronchodilator spirometry confirms persistent obstruction. Treatment Stopping smoking is the most important step. Inhalers, vaccines, pulmonary rehabilitation and oxygen for selected patients can improve outcomes. Exacerbations Sudden worsening of breathlessness or sputum may require urgent medical treatment. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. References and further reading CDC and MedlinePlus condition overviews NIDDK endocrine, diabetes and digestive disease resources NHS condition guides Alberta and international emergency-care principles Quick facts Abbreviation: COPD Includes chronic bronchitis and emphysema Usually diagnosed with spirometry Emergency: In Qatar, call 999 for severe or sudden life-threatening symptoms. description: MedMinute medical education platform
text: Clinical Skills | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Clinical › Understand the tools and skills used in healthcare. Clinical & Nursing Skills Library Understand the tools and skills used in healthcare. 5 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 Explore vital signs, diagnostics, nursing care, procedures and safety practices. + Vital Signs & Nursing Assessment 5 topics + Diagnostics & Monitoring 5 topics + Procedures & Patient Care 5 topics + Emergency & Safety Skills 5 topics Choose a skill Select a topic to view its purpose, equipment, professional roles and key safety points. Blood Pressure Measurement Heart Rate and Pulse Respiratory Rate Pulse Oximetry Temperature Electrocardiogram (ECG) Echocardiogram Spirometry EEG Blood Tests Venipuncture IV Cannulation Wound Dressing Splinting Medication Administration Basic Life Support Triage Infection Control Patient Identification Clinical Documentation Vital Signs & Nursing Assessment Blood Pressure Measurement Purpose Measures the force of blood against artery walls. Equipment Cuff and stethoscope or automated monitor Who performs it? Nurses, physicians and trained healthcare staff Important point Correct cuff size, resting position and repeated readings improve accuracy. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. Vital Signs & Nursing Assessment Heart Rate and Pulse Purpose Assesses rate, rhythm and pulse strength. Equipment Clock or monitor Who performs it? Nurses, physicians, paramedics and other clinicians Important point An irregular pulse may require ECG confirmation. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. Vital Signs & Nursing Assessment Respiratory Rate Purpose Counts breaths per minute and observes breathing pattern. Equipment Observation and clock Who performs it? Nurses and clinical staff Important point It can change early when a patient is deteriorating. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. Vital Signs & Nursing Assessment Pulse Oximetry Purpose Estimates blood oxygen saturation. Equipment Pulse oximeter Who performs it? Nurses, physicians and respiratory therapists Important point Cold fingers, movement and nail products can affect readings. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. Vital Signs & Nursing Assessment Temperature Purpose Assesses fever or abnormal body temperature. Equipment Digital thermometer Who performs it? Nurses and clinical staff Important point Different measurement sites have different normal ranges. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. Diagnostics & Monitoring Electrocardiogram (ECG) Purpose Records the heart's electrical activity. Equipment Skin electrodes and ECG machine Who performs it? Nurses or technicians acquire it; clinicians interpret it Important point Used for chest pain, arrhythmias, fainting and many cardiac problems. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. Diagnostics & Monitoring Echocardiogram Purpose Uses ultrasound to visualize heart structure and function. Equipment Ultrasound machine Who performs it? Cardiac sonographers and cardiologists Important point Shows pumping function, valves and blood flow. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. Diagnostics & Monitoring Spirometry Purpose Measures airflow and lung volumes. Equipment Spirometer Who performs it? Respiratory therapists, technicians and clinicians Important point Important in asthma and COPD assessment. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. Diagnostics & Monitoring EEG Purpose Records electrical activity from the brain. Equipment Scalp electrodes and EEG system Who performs it? Neurophysiology technologists and neurologists Important point Used in seizure evaluation and selected neurological conditions. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. Diagnostics & Monitoring Blood Tests Purpose Analyze cells, chemistry, hormones and markers. Equipment Needles, tubes and laboratory systems Who performs it? Phlebotomists, nurses and laboratory professionals Important point Results must be interpreted in clinical context. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. Procedures & Patient Care Venipuncture Purpose Collects blood from a vein for testing. Equipment Tourniquet, needle, tubes and antiseptic Who performs it? Phlebotomists, nurses and trained clinicians Important point Patient identity and correct tube labeling are essential. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. Procedures & Patient Care IV Cannulation Purpose Places a small catheter into a vein. Equipment Cannula, tourniquet, antiseptic and dressing Who performs it? Nurses, physicians, paramedics and trained staff Important point Used for fluids, medication or contrast. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. Procedures & Patient Care Wound Dressing Purpose Protects a wound and supports healing. Equipment Gloves, cleansing materials and dressing Who performs it? Nurses and other trained clinicians Important point Technique depends on wound type and infection risk. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. Procedures & Patient Care Splinting Purpose Temporarily stabilizes an injured limb. Equipment Splint, padding and bandage Who performs it? Emergency staff, nurses, physicians and paramedics Important point Circulation and sensation must be checked before and after. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. Procedures & Patient Care Medication Administration Purpose Safely gives prescribed medication. Equipment Medication record and route-specific equipment Who performs it? Nurses, physicians and pharmacists in defined roles Important point Correct patient, medication, dose, route and time are core checks. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. Emergency & Safety Skills Basic Life Support Purpose Provides chest compressions and ventilation during cardiac arrest. Equipment CPR training and AED when available Who performs it? Trained responders and healthcare professionals Important point Immediate action and early defibrillation can save lives. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. Emergency & Safety Skills Triage Purpose Prioritizes patients by urgency. Equipment Clinical assessment system Who performs it? Emergency nurses and physicians Important point The sickest patient is treated first, not necessarily the first to arrive. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. Emergency & Safety Skills Infection Control Purpose Reduces spread of infectious organisms. Equipment Hand hygiene, PPE and isolation procedures Who performs it? All healthcare workers Important point Hand hygiene is one of the most effective safety measures. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. Emergency & Safety Skills Patient Identification Purpose Confirms the correct patient before care. Equipment Identity band and verbal confirmation Who performs it? All healthcare workers Important point At least two identifiers are commonly used. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. Emergency & Safety Skills Clinical Documentation Purpose Creates an accurate record of care. Equipment Electronic or paper health record Who performs it? All regulated healthcare professionals Important point Documentation should be timely, objective and clear. This page explains the skill conceptually. Clinical procedures require approved training, supervision and institutional policy. title: Concussion
description: A mild traumatic brain injury that can affect thinking, balance, mood and sleep.
text: Concussion | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Concussion Sports Medicine Concussion 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 A mild traumatic brain injury that can affect thinking, balance, mood and sleep. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is A concussion results from biomechanical forces that temporarily disrupt brain function. Symptoms Headache, dizziness, nausea, light sensitivity, slowed thinking, irritability and sleep change. Diagnosis Diagnosis is clinical. Routine scans are usually normal and are used mainly to exclude more serious injury. Recovery A brief period of relative rest followed by gradual return to learning and activity is commonly advised. Red flags Repeated vomiting, worsening headache, seizure, weakness, confusion or unusual drowsiness requires emergency assessment. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. References and further reading CDC and MedlinePlus condition overviews NIDDK endocrine, diabetes and digestive disease resources NHS condition guides Alberta and international emergency-care principles Quick facts Can occur without loss of consciousness Return to sport should be gradual Repeat injury before recovery is dangerous Emergency: In Qatar, call 999 for severe or sudden life-threatening symptoms. title: Coronary Artery Disease
description: How plaque narrows the arteries supplying the heart and why prevention and early treatment matter.
text: Coronary Artery Disease | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Coronary Artery Disease Cardiology Coronary Artery Disease 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 How plaque narrows the arteries supplying the heart and why prevention and early treatment matter. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is Coronary artery disease develops when fatty deposits and inflammation cause plaque to build inside arteries that supply the heart muscle. Symptoms Some people have no symptoms. Others experience chest pressure, shortness of breath, unusual fatigue or discomfort spreading to the arm, jaw, shoulder or back. Diagnosis Assessment may include an ECG, blood tests, exercise or imaging-based stress testing, coronary CT angiography or invasive angiography. Treatment and prevention Treatment may include lifestyle changes, medicines to lower cholesterol and blood pressure, antiplatelet therapy when indicated, and procedures such as angioplasty, stenting or bypass surgery. When it is urgent New or severe chest pressure, sweating, nausea, breathlessness or faintness can signal a heart attack and requires emergency care. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. References and further reading CDC and MedlinePlus condition overviews NIDDK endocrine, diabetes and digestive disease resources NHS condition guides Alberta and international emergency-care principles Quick facts Also called: coronary heart disease Key symptom: chest pressure with exertion Major risks: smoking, diabetes, high blood pressure, high cholesterol Emergency: In Qatar, call 999 for severe or sudden life-threatening symptoms. Vascular Medicine Endocrine gland A gland that releases hormones into the bloodstream. Endocrinology Endoscopy Use of a camera-equipped instrument to view internal structures. Gastroenterology Enzyme A biological catalyst that speeds chemical reactions. Biochemistry Etiology The cause or origin of a disease. Nephrology Glucose A simple sugar used as a major cellular fuel. Endocrinology Hemoglobin Oxygen-carrying protein inside red blood cells. Hematology Hemorrhage Bleeding from a damaged blood vessel. description: Understanding recurrent unprovoked seizures, diagnosis and modern treatment.
text: Epilepsy | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is Epilepsy is a neurological condition characterized by a lasting tendency to have seizures. Seizures Seizures can cause convulsions, staring, altered awareness, unusual sensations or brief abnormal movements. Diagnosis History from the patient and witnesses is essential. EEG and brain imaging may help identify type and cause. Treatment Anti-seizure medicines are the main treatment. Selected patients may benefit from surgery, devices or dietary therapy. First aid Protect the person from injury, time the seizure, place them on their side when safe and do not put anything in their mouth. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. References and further reading CDC and MedlinePlus condition overviews NIDDK endocrine, diabetes and digestive disease resources NHS condition guides Alberta and international emergency-care principles Quick facts Diagnosis usually requires recurrent unprovoked seizures or high recurrence risk Many seizure types exist Treatment is individualized Emergency: In Qatar, call 999 for severe or sudden life-threatening symptoms. title: Saad Aboumalik
description: MedMinute medical education platform
text: Meet the Founder | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Founder › Saad Aboumalik Meet the Founder Saad Aboumalik 2 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 Founder of MedMinute, Grade 12 student at Blyth Academy Qatar and aspiring physician. Founder photograph can be added later Why I started MedMinute I created MedMinute because medical information often feels harder to understand than it needs to be. Students and families may hear unfamiliar terms, receive laboratory results or experience symptoms without knowing how the pieces connect. MedMinute aims to turn complex ideas into clear, responsible educational explanations. The project is not intended to diagnose illness or replace healthcare professionals. Its purpose is to help people become more informed, ask better questions and feel less intimidated by medicine. My journey The experiences that shaped MedMinute. 01 Interest in science and medicine Biology, chemistry and the relationship between the body's systems developed into a strong interest in medicine and endocrinology. 02 March 2025: hypothyroidism diagnosis After experiencing fatigue, lethargy, weakness, brain fog and reduced performance in school and sports, I was diagnosed with hypothyroidism. Understanding tests such as TSH and free T4, and learning which specialists manage thyroid disease, took time. 03 Learning independently Because the symptoms were initially difficult to connect, I spent significant time learning how the thyroid works and how hormone disorders can affect energy, mood, concentration and daily life. 04 Hospital internship My hospital experience gave me direct exposure to healthcare teamwork, patient communication, professionalism and the importance of accurate observation. 05 Health Education Club in progress I am working toward developing a school-based health education initiative that can make science and healthcare topics more engaging for students. 06 Creating MedMinute MedMinute brings these interests together through medical articles, Alberta curriculum study support, career guides, clinical skills, interactive cases and public-health education. Why education matters Health education can reduce confusion, improve communication and help people recognize when persistent or urgent symptoms deserve professional attention. Clear information also allows students to explore healthcare careers more realistically. Future plans Over the coming months, I hope to publish more original articles, create educational videos, speak with healthcare professionals, develop Arabic resources and expand opportunities for students interested in medicine. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is In Graves' disease, stimulating antibodies activate the TSH receptor, causing excessive thyroid hormone production. Symptoms Palpitations, tremor, heat intolerance, sweating, anxiety, weight loss and muscle weakness. Eye irritation or bulging can occur. title: Hashimoto's Disease
description: An autoimmune attack on the thyroid that commonly causes hypothyroidism.
text: Hashimoto's Disease | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Hashimoto's Disease Autoimmune & Rheumatology Hashimoto's Disease 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 An autoimmune attack on the thyroid that commonly causes hypothyroidism. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is Hashimoto's disease occurs when immune cells and antibodies target the thyroid, gradually reducing its ability to make hormones. Symptoms Symptoms usually reflect hypothyroidism: fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, slowed thinking and menstrual changes. Diagnosis TSH and free T4 assess function. Anti-TPO or thyroglobulin antibodies may support the autoimmune cause. Treatment Levothyroxine replaces deficient thyroid hormone. Antibody levels are not usually used to adjust the dose. Monitoring Needs can change with growth, pregnancy, weight change, medication interactions and time. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. title: Heart Attack
description: What happens when blood flow to heart muscle is suddenly blocked, common warning signs and emergency treatment.
text: Heart Attack | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Heart Attack Cardiology Heart Attack 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 What happens when blood flow to heart muscle is suddenly blocked, common warning signs and emergency treatment. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What happens A heart attack occurs when a coronary artery becomes blocked long enough to injure or kill part of the heart muscle. Possible symptoms Pressure, heaviness or pain in the chest; shortness of breath; sweating; nausea; unusual fatigue; or discomfort in the jaw, back or arm. Symptoms can vary. Diagnosis Clinicians use the history, ECG changes and cardiac troponin blood testing. Imaging or coronary angiography may follow. Treatment Rapid treatment may include antiplatelet medicines and urgent restoration of blood flow through angioplasty and a stent. Other medicines reduce strain on the heart and prevent recurrence. Emergency action Do not drive yourself. In Qatar, call 999 for suspected heart attack. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. References and further reading CDC and MedlinePlus condition overviews NIDDK endocrine, diabetes and digestive disease resources NHS condition guides Alberta and international emergency-care principles Quick facts Medical term: myocardial infarction Emergency condition Diagnosis: ECG and cardiac troponin Emergency: In Qatar, call 999 for severe or sudden life-threatening symptoms. title: Heart Failure

text: Heart Failure | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Heart Failure Cardiology Heart Failure 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 A guide to a condition in which the heart cannot pump or fill efficiently enough to meet the body's needs. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is Heart failure means the heart is unable to pump enough blood or fill normally. It may develop after coronary disease, high blood pressure, valve disease, cardiomyopathy or other conditions. Symptoms Breathlessness during activity or while lying flat, ankle swelling, rapid weight gain from fluid, persistent fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance. Diagnosis Evaluation often includes an ECG, blood tests, chest imaging and an echocardiogram to assess heart structure and pumping function. Treatment Treatment depends on the type and cause and may include several evidence-based medicines, reduced dietary sodium when advised, diuretics, devices or surgery. Living well Daily weight monitoring and recognizing worsening swelling or breathlessness can help identify fluid accumulation early. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. title: Hypertension
description: Why high blood pressure is often silent and how it affects the heart, brain, kidneys and blood vessels.
text: Hypertension | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Hypertension Cardiology Hypertension 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 Why high blood pressure is often silent and how it affects the heart, brain, kidneys and blood vessels. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is Hypertension means blood pressure remains above the recommended range over time. Why it matters Long-term pressure damages artery walls and increases the risk of heart attack, stroke, kidney disease, eye damage and heart failure. Diagnosis Diagnosis should use correct cuff size, proper technique and repeated measurements. Home or ambulatory monitoring can clarify uncertain readings. Treatment Regular activity, a balanced lower-sodium eating pattern, healthy weight, avoiding tobacco and taking prescribed medication can reduce risk. Important note A single high reading during stress or illness is not always enough to diagnose chronic hypertension. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. title: Rheumatoid Arthritis
description: An autoimmune inflammatory arthritis that can damage joints and affect other organs.
text: Rheumatoid Arthritis | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Rheumatoid Arthritis Autoimmune & Rheumatology Rheumatoid Arthritis 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 An autoimmune inflammatory arthritis that can damage joints and affect other organs. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is Rheumatoid arthritis causes persistent inflammation of the joint lining. Symptoms Painful swollen joints, prolonged morning stiffness, fatigue and reduced grip strength. Diagnosis Clinical examination, inflammatory markers, rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP antibodies and imaging can support diagnosis. Treatment Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs are started early to control inflammation and prevent damage. Beyond joints The condition can also affect lungs, eyes, blood vessels and cardiovascular risk. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. References and further reading CDC and MedlinePlus condition overviews NIDDK endocrine, diabetes and digestive disease resources NHS condition guides Alberta and international emergency-care principles Quick facts Often affects small joints symmetrically Morning stiffness is common Early treatment protects joints Emergency: In Qatar, call 999 for severe or sudden life-threatening symptoms. title: Seasonal Influenza
description: A contagious viral respiratory infection that can cause serious complications in high-risk people.
text: Seasonal Influenza | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Seasonal Influenza Family Medicine Seasonal Influenza 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 A contagious viral respiratory infection that can cause serious complications in high-risk people. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is Influenza usually causes abrupt fever, muscle aches, headache, cough and fatigue. Spread It spreads mainly through respiratory particles and close contact. Treatment Rest, fluids and symptom relief are enough for many people. Antiviral medicine is most useful when started early in eligible patients. Prevention Annual vaccination, staying home while acutely ill and hand and respiratory hygiene reduce spread. Urgent signs Breathing difficulty, chest pain, confusion, dehydration or worsening after improvement requires prompt care. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. References and further reading CDC and MedlinePlus condition overviews NIDDK endocrine, diabetes and digestive disease resources NHS condition guides Alberta and international emergency-care principles Quick facts Caused by influenza viruses Vaccination reduces risk Antibiotics do not treat influenza Emergency: In Qatar, call 999 for severe or sudden life-threatening symptoms. Training pathway Stage 1: Medical degree Complete medical school and licensing. Stage 2: Anesthesiology residency Train in airway management, physiology, pharmacology, intensive care and anesthesia for many surgical fields. Stage 3: Optional fellowship Subspecialize in pain medicine, cardiac anesthesia, obstetric anesthesia, pediatric anesthesia or critical care. Important: The exact route and training length differ between Qatar, Ireland, the United Kingdom, Canada, the United States and other systems. What the daily work actually involves Assess patients before surgery and identify anesthesia risks. Create an individualized anesthetic plan. Manage airway, breathing, circulation, pain and consciousness during procedures. Respond rapidly to changes in blood pressure, oxygen or heart rhythm. Supervise recovery and postoperative pain control. Typical day: The anesthesiologist is continuously monitoring physiology and making rapid adjustments, even when the patient appears stable. Common conditions and responsibilities Perioperative risk Difficult airway Acute and chronic pain Critical illness Obstetric and pediatric anesthesia needs Emergency resuscitation Who may enjoy this specialty? The field combines careful clinical assessment with advanced testing, medications and procedures. Training pathway Stage 1: Medical degree Complete medical school and medical licensing. Stage 2: Internal medicine residency Build broad expertise in adult medical care and acute illness. Stage 3: Cardiology fellowship Train in ECG interpretation, echocardiography, coronary disease, heart failure, rhythm disorders and cardiac procedures. Manage coronary artery disease, heart failure, valve disease and arrhythmias. Perform or assist with procedures such as angiography, stenting or rhythm studies, depending on subspecialty. Work closely with emergency physicians, surgeons, radiologists and intensive-care teams. Typical day: A cardiologist may begin with hospital rounds, move to clinic, review heart imaging and later perform a procedure or respond to an emergency consultation. Common conditions and responsibilities Coronary artery disease and heart attack Atrial fibrillation and other arrhythmias Heart failure Valve disease Cardiomyopathy Hypertension and inherited heart conditions Who may enjoy this specialty? Training pathway Stage 1: Medical degree Complete medical school and licensing. Stage 2: Internal medicine or pediatrics residency Develop broad medical expertise. Stage 3: Infectious diseases fellowship Train in microbiology, antimicrobial therapy, hospital infection, travel medicine and immune-compromised patients. Discuss findings with treating clinicians. Perform ultrasound, biopsy, drainage or vascular procedures in some roles. Prioritize emergency imaging for stroke, trauma and internal bleeding. Typical day: A radiologist's day combines focused image interpretation, consultation and, in procedural fields, direct patient care. title: Stroke
description: How interruption of blood flow or bleeding in the brain causes sudden neurological symptoms.
text: Stroke | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › Stroke Neurology Stroke 1 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 How interruption of blood flow or bleeding in the brain causes sudden neurological symptoms. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is An ischemic stroke results from blocked blood flow to part of the brain. A hemorrhagic stroke results from bleeding in or around the brain. Warning signs Sudden facial droop, arm weakness, speech difficulty, vision loss, imbalance, severe headache or numbness on one side. Diagnosis Urgent brain imaging distinguishes bleeding from blockage because treatment differs. Treatment Eligible ischemic strokes may be treated with clot-dissolving medicine or mechanical thrombectomy. Hemorrhagic stroke treatment focuses on controlling bleeding, pressure and the cause. Emergency action Use FAST: Face, Arm, Speech, Time. In Qatar, call 999 immediately. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. title: The Thyroid Gland: Why One Small Organ Can Affect Your Entire Life
description: How thyroid hormones influence energy, metabolism, concentration and mood, including hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, testing and treatment.
text: The Thyroid Gland: Why One Small Organ Can Affect Your Entire Life | MedMinute MedMinute 4.3 introduces the redesigned professional Anatomy Explorer. View Live Impact Global Search Search all of MedMinute × Home › Health Library › The Thyroid Gland: Why One Small Organ Can Affect Your Entire Life Endocrinology The Thyroid Gland: Why One Small Organ Can Affect Your Entire Life 2 min estimated Last updated July 15, 2026 How thyroid hormones influence energy, metabolism, concentration and mood, including hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, testing and treatment. Educational purpose: General information only; not a diagnosis or substitute for medical care. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud Why this matters In March 2025, MedMinute founder Saad Aboumalik was diagnosed with hypothyroidism after persistent fatigue, weakness, brain fog, low energy and reduced performance in school and sports. Reaching a diagnosis required learning which tests to request and which specialists manage thyroid disease. That experience helped inspire MedMinute: clear health education cannot diagnose disease, but it can help people recognize persistent changes and ask informed questions sooner. Where the thyroid is located The thyroid is a small, butterfly-shaped gland at the front of the neck, below the voice box and in front of the trachea. Its two lobes are joined by a narrow bridge of tissue called the isthmus. How it works The thyroid makes thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3). The pituitary gland releases thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), which signals the thyroid to produce hormones. These hormones influence how cells use energy and therefore affect heart rate, temperature, digestion, muscle function, growth, concentration and mood. Hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism Hypothyroidism means the gland produces too little hormone, which can cause fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, slowed thinking and weight gain. Hyperthyroidism means excessive hormone production and may cause palpitations, heat intolerance, tremor, anxiety, sweating, sleep difficulty and weight loss. Diagnosis Testing usually begins with TSH and free T4. Free T3 is particularly helpful when hyperthyroidism is suspected. Thyroid antibody tests may support diagnoses such as Hashimoto’s disease or Graves’ disease. Ultrasound or nuclear medicine testing is used only when clinically appropriate. Treatment Hypothyroidism is commonly treated with levothyroxine, with the dose adjusted using repeat blood testing. Hyperthyroidism may be treated with antithyroid medication, radioactive iodine or surgery, depending on the cause and the patient. Thyroid disease is usually managed rather than described as having one universal cure. MedMinute takeaway Persistent, severe or worsening symptoms should be discussed with a qualified healthcare professional. References and further reading CDC and MedlinePlus condition overviews NIDDK endocrine, diabetes and digestive disease resources NHS condition guides Alberta and international emergency-care principles Quick facts Main hormones: T4 and T3 First-line test: TSH Specialist: Endocrinology Common treatment for hypothyroidism: levothyroxine Emergency: In Qatar, call 999 for severe or sudden life-threatening symptoms. Approximately 2 min read Last updated July 15, 2026 🔊 Read article aloud What it is A thoracic aortic aneurysm is enlargement of the section of the aorta that passes through the chest. Causes and risk Age, hypertension and atherosclerosis are common contributors. Some cases are linked to bicuspid aortic valve or inherited connective-tissue and aortic conditions.
